segments.title: Tillman shapes the conference without being a delegate
segments.summary: The scenario places Benjamin Tillman in Washington as a behind-the-scenes Senate power broker rather than a formal treaty delegate.
segments.body: Tillman's preferred methods were private leverage, opposition research, and negotiated favors rather than public diplomacy. Dr. Clarke therefore expects him to move through the conference using Senate influence and a personal agenda, changing which compromises could survive ratification.

evidence.note: The transcript distinguishes a formal delegation role from Tillman's preference for private political leverage.

segments.title: A battleship ceiling clears the way for cruisers
segments.summary: Tillman's giant battleship proposals are interpreted as a way to end incremental escalation and redirect American construction toward cruisers.
segments.body: The objective was not endless construction of enormous battleships, but a maximum standard that would make the next marginal capital-ship improvement unnecessary. A stable ceiling would release money and yard capacity for the cruiser force the United States had neglected while building battleships.

evidence.note: The transcript connects Tillman's maximum-battleship idea with ending incremental escalation and restarting cruiser production.

segments.title: Charleston Navy Yard anchors Tillman's strategy
segments.summary: Charleston's cruiser-sized facilities turn naval procurement into jobs, electoral leverage, and a durable South Carolina power base.
segments.body: Tillman secured a yard whose slips suited destroyers, submarines, and cruisers rather than the largest capital ships. The scenario treats his support for particular programs and builders as a patronage system: construction mattered most when it strengthened South Carolina or banked favors with politically useful states.

evidence.note: The transcript describes Charleston's yard dimensions and connects Tillman's procurement choices to South Carolina employment and political favors.

segments.title: HMS Hood pushes the ceiling above 35,000 tons
segments.summary: Tillman's experience with maximum battleships makes the historical 35,000-ton limit implausible while HMS Hood remains a 41,000-ton benchmark.
segments.body: A senator who had studied ships approaching twice the historical treaty limit would not readily accept 35,000 tons as an adequate maximum. Hood's accepted treaty displacement becomes the bargaining reference, while the Panama Canal constrains American escalation more severely than larger yards or a dredged Suez constrain Britain.

evidence.note: The transcript uses Tillman's large designs, Hood's treaty displacement, and canal infrastructure to argue for a higher ceiling.

segments.title: Tillman must survive long enough to embed the bargain
segments.summary: The counterfactual requires Tillman to win reelection and remain influential until roughly 1928, not merely live to see the conference.
segments.body: Surviving the 1918 primary would probably return Tillman to a safely Democratic Senate seat. Dr. Clarke extends his life another decade because implementing a treaty takes one term and making its political and industrial settlement durable often takes another.

evidence.note: The transcript explains the election assumption and why Tillman must remain active through treaty implementation and another election cycle.

segments.title: Why carriers cannot replace battleships in the 1920s
segments.summary: A viewer's carrier-first idea is tested against the limited striking power and doctrine of early interwar naval aviation.
segments.body: British carrier aviation was comparatively advanced, but its aircraft still offered limited independent striking power. Carrier aircraft were especially valuable for spotting battleship gunfire, so abandoning the battle line in the early 1920s would leave a navy vulnerable before aviation matured.
segments.question: Could Tillman's scenario shift resources from battleships to aircraft carriers?
segments.answerShort: Not as a substitute for battleships in the 1920s: early carrier aircraft were not yet decisive, and their most important fleet role was often spotting for capital-ship guns.

evidence.note: The transcript answers a carrier-first suggestion by comparing early aircraft capability with contemporary battleships.

segments.title: The Omaha orders reveal the first divergence
segments.summary: Tillman's less naval-minded successors let Charleston miss the Omaha program; his survival would almost certainly redirect cruiser orders there.
segments.body: The senators who followed Tillman concentrated on banking and law rather than using naval appropriations to build South Carolina industry. Because no Omaha-class cruiser went to Charleston, the class becomes the clearest early change: a surviving Tillman would demand local hulls and steer work toward Democratic constituencies.

evidence.note: The transcript contrasts Tillman with his successors and maps Omaha construction to yards outside Charleston.

segments.title: A jobs coalition gains power as talks drag on
segments.summary: A year-long conference rewards Tillman's backroom skills and gives a dozen pro-construction senators leverage over any treaty requiring ratification.
segments.body: Tillman could cooperate with British, Japanese, French, or Italian negotiators whenever doing so advanced his domestic interests. His faction's common objective is employment: ships sustain yard jobs, jobs protect elections, and a disciplined Senate bloc can trade acceptance of a treaty for continued construction.

evidence.note: The transcript connects the conference's duration, Tillman's cross-delegation pragmatism, and a Senate coalition organized around shipbuilding employment.

segments.title: Shipyard politics decide which capital ships survive
segments.summary: Tillman's domestic target list follows allied senators, swing states, and working shipyards rather than a neutral ranking of hull completion.
segments.body: Virginia Senator Claude Swanson gives Newport News and Norfolk powerful protection, while California and North Carolina offer politically valuable slips. Tillman can present his eventual acceptance of the treaty as a concession to Republicans while ensuring that Democratic yards either finish ships or receive replacement work.

evidence.note: The transcript maps Lexington, Colorado, and South Dakota hulls to shipyards, senators, and electoral targets.

segments.title: Anglo-American competition stops short of war planning
segments.summary: Shared shipyard leadership and constructor exchanges make the interwar naval rivalry a prestige contest more than evidence of an approaching war.
segments.body: British and American naval industries exchanged managers, designers, and technical knowledge throughout the period. Both governments kept contingency plans because the other was the only comparable naval power, but the lack of serious intelligence collection and their mutual economic usefulness point to a qualitative race for prestige rather than preparations for conflict.

evidence.note: The transcript cites transatlantic shipyard personnel, constructor exchanges, and limited intelligence effort to characterize the rivalry.

segments.title: Why technical exchanges still missed British armor
segments.summary: A question about British armor shows that access to allied designers does not automatically reveal a capability no one thinks to investigate.
segments.body: American designers had considered related armor ideas and dismissed them, so they did not ask whether Britain had made the concept work. The same pattern applied to torpedoes and layouts: exchanges moved knowledge only when participants recognized the question worth asking.
segments.question: How did the United States miss British water-based armor despite constructor exchanges?
segments.answerShort: American designers did not ask whether Britain had solved a concept they had already judged impractical, illustrating the limits of informal technical exchange without focused intelligence requirements.

evidence.note: The transcript directly answers why constructor exchanges failed to reveal the armor system and compares other missed technologies.

segments.title: War debts do not make Anglo-American war likely
segments.summary: Calling British wartime debt would damage American interests and force the United States to confront maritime burdens it preferred Britain to carry.
segments.body: The debt was smaller than later Second World War obligations and sat inside a wider web of Allied lending and trade. Britain could resist an improper demand, while America still benefited from the Royal Navy carrying much of the cost of policing global commerce.
segments.question: Could American pressure over British war debt trigger a conflict?
segments.answerShort: It was unlikely because coercion would disrupt trade and finance while leaving the United States with global policing burdens it did not want in the 1920s.

evidence.note: The transcript weighs war debt, trade, financial enforcement, and America's reluctance to become the world's policeman.

segments.title: The Hawkins class exposes America's cruiser gap
segments.summary: A visit to a Hawkins-class cruiser would give Tillman a concrete foreign benchmark and ammunition for a hostile Senate hearing on American design.
segments.body: The Hawkins class helped define the historical treaty cruiser even though many negotiators had never inspected one. Dr. Clarke imagines Tillman turning an in-person comparison with the Omaha class into pressure on the Bureau of Construction and Repair for a larger, continuing American cruiser program.

evidence.note: The transcript identifies Hawkins as the cruiser benchmark and describes the likely Senate response to its superiority over Omaha.

segments.title: Three South Dakotas reshape the American settlement
segments.summary: The revised treaty completes three politically selected South Dakotas, retains three Colorados, and retires older ships under a 40,000-ton ceiling.
segments.body: Montana, North Carolina, and Iowa preserve work at yards linked to Democratic priorities even though they were not simply the three most complete hulls. Together with three Colorados, they give America six 16-inch-gun ships while Florida and Utah are sacrificed to keep aggregate tonnage within the bargain.

evidence.note: The transcript names the retained South Dakotas, gives their completion percentages, and explains the 40,000-ton and 16-inch limits.

segments.title: Japan trades older hulls for Tosa or Amagi
segments.summary: A 360,000-ton Japanese allowance creates several plausible exchanges between Kongo-class ships and the unfinished Tosa or Amagi designs.
segments.body: Japan could retire a Kongo to complete two Tosas, mix a Tosa with an Amagi, or pursue another combination near the 40,000-ton limit. The earthquake risk to Amagi remains, making the final fleet and carrier-conversion pool contingent rather than a single certain outcome.

evidence.note: The transcript calculates Japanese tonnage choices involving Kongo, Tosa, and Amagi.

segments.title: Britain can build a squadron of F3-like fast battleships
segments.summary: A 600,000-ton allowance gives Britain several paths to five or six new capital ships without sacrificing its 15-inch battle line.
segments.body: Britain can discard older 13.5-inch ships while retaining the Queen Elizabeths and Revenges, then choose five roughly 38,000-ton F3-like ships or a mixed battleship and battlecruiser program. More length and displacement buy speed, magazine separation, and less compressed machinery than the historical Nelson design.

evidence.note: The transcript calculates British retained tonnage and alternative five- or six-ship construction programs.
The transcript compares Nelson and F3 layouts and considers battleship and battlecruiser gun choices.

segments.title: Why Britain cannot evade limits through Dominion transfers
segments.summary: The treaty prevents Britain from simply handing surplus battleships to Dominion navies, though Tiger could plausibly serve with Australia inside the British allocation.
segments.body: Canada, India, and South Africa lacked either the political appetite or naval structure to absorb major capital ships. Australia was the credible operator, and an older 13.5-inch battlecruiser such as Tiger would provide regional presence without the provocation of transferring a modern 15-inch ship.
segments.question: Could Britain distribute surplus capital ships among Dominion navies instead of scrapping them?
segments.answerShort: Not outside the treaty allocation; Australia might operate Tiger on Britain's behalf, but transferring modern ships could not be used to escape the limits.

evidence.note: The transcript answers the Dominion-transfer question and assesses which navies could operate a British capital ship.

segments.title: Four Lexington conversions create demand for escorts
segments.summary: The alternate treaty doubles carrier allowances, preserves four Lexington hulls, and limits their guns to six inches to protect cruiser demand.
segments.body: Lexington and Saratoga remain difficult to cancel, while Constellation and Ranger preserve Virginia work important to Tillman's Senate ally. Restricting all four conversions to six-inch guns prevents them from replacing their own cruiser screen and may redirect Omaha turret work toward carrier armament contracts.

evidence.note: The transcript sets the larger carrier allowance, four conversion exception, six-inch gun limit, and political reasons for preserving specific Lexington hulls.

segments.title: Cruisers serve trade, scouting, and steady employment
segments.summary: Tillman's cruiser preference combines genuine naval missions with the longer, steadier labor demand of a major Charleston hull.
segments.body: Cruisers protect overseas trade and scout for the battle fleet without requiring America to assume Britain's global policing role. Politically, they also provide more stable employment than destroyers, whose short construction cycles produce sharper peaks and troughs in yard labor.
segments.question: Did Tillman want cruisers only because Charleston could build them?
segments.answerShort: No. He also valued trade protection and fleet reconnaissance, although cruisers' sustained employment at Charleston made them especially attractive.

evidence.note: The transcript directly answers the question by listing trade, scouting, ship size, and employment continuity.

segments.title: Navies would spend carrier tonnage gradually
segments.summary: The larger carrier allowance would not produce ten immediate hulls because conversions take years and aviation designs continue to evolve.
segments.body: Most navies historically used substantial treaty tonnage while reserving room for later designs. Four Lexington conversions would not enter service until roughly 1927-28, already bringing the next treaty conference close enough to encourage a staged program.
segments.question: Would navies consume their new carrier tonnage immediately or save it for later?
segments.answerShort: They would probably mix early conversions with reserved capacity, because large carriers take years to finish and later designs may use the remaining tonnage better.

evidence.note: The transcript compares how navies use cumulative limits and dates the Lexington conversions against the next treaty.

segments.title: Japan must choose between battleships and carrier conversions
segments.summary: Japanese treaty gains collide with limited laid-down hulls, cumulative carrier tonnage, and the earthquake that historically destroyed Amagi.
segments.body: Four 33,000-ton conversions would consume almost all Japan's 162,000-ton allowance, yet the later Amagi hulls were barely begun. Completing Tosa-class battleships narrows the conversion pool further, and losing Amagi to the earthquake could leave Japan with three large carriers rather than four.

evidence.note: The transcript calculates Japanese conversion tonnage, identifies available hulls, and applies the earthquake to Amagi.

segments.title: A 12,000-ton cruiser keeps yards and designers busy
segments.summary: Raising the cruiser ceiling to 12,000 tons creates an immediate break from Omaha and invites twelve-gun heavy-cruiser designs.
segments.body: The extra displacement makes a new program politically defensible and keeps qualitative competition alive rather than settling quickly on a single 10,000-ton pattern. British County and American Pensacola descendants could carry twelve 8-inch guns, making German pocket battleships far less comfortable commerce raiders.

evidence.note: The transcript derives a 12,000-ton cruiser limit from Charleston capacity and explores twelve-gun British and American designs.

segments.title: The loophole becomes a light-carrier category
segments.summary: Widespread sub-12,000-ton carriers would likely be regulated as a distinct type rather than eliminated as a loophole.
segments.body: Britain could separate small trade-protection carriers from larger fleet strike carriers, while the United States might combine four Lexingtons with six enlarged Yorktown-like ships. If every power exploits the threshold, later negotiators are more likely to define a light-carrier category than prohibit it entirely.

evidence.note: The transcript discusses Hermes-sized carriers, American fleet-carrier numbers, and how a widely used loophole would become a treaty category.

segments.title: Continuous shipbuilding could soften the Depression
segments.summary: A larger treaty fleet keeps shipyards, suppliers, and wages active, giving Britain and America an industrial cushion after the Wall Street Crash.
segments.body: Capital ships, cruisers, and small carriers would sustain skilled yards that historically lost work. Dr. Clarke argues that defense and infrastructure orders circulate wages through the wider economy, making a downturn easier to manage even though protectionism would still damage export-dependent Germany.

evidence.note: The transcript links sustained naval construction with employment, circulating money, and greater resilience after the 1929 crash.

segments.title: More carriers reshape doctrine and aircraft production
segments.summary: A deeper interwar carrier program preserves yards, expands aircraft orders, and lets Britain combine larger air groups with armored protection sooner.
segments.body: Continuous construction through the 1930s would create more experienced carrier yards and a larger naval-aircraft industry. Britain might keep the Fleet Air Arm under naval control earlier and develop Ark Royal-sized groups with Illustrious-style subdivision, increasing the striking power available for operations such as Taranto.

evidence.note: The transcript connects continuing carrier construction to aircraft orders, Fleet Air Arm control, armor, air-group size, and Taranto.

segments.title: Italy gains several Caracciolo-class battleships
segments.summary: Italy's larger allowance could complete three Francesco Caracciolo-class ships, worsening France's treaty position and strengthening the Mediterranean fleet.
segments.body: After retaining its desired older ships, Italy would still have enough room for multiple fast Caracciolo-class completions under the 40,000-ton limit. France's equal paper ratio would be less useful because its government remained less willing to fund construction, while Japan's infrastructure would still cap how far it could exploit its own gains.

evidence.note: The transcript calculates Italian retained tonnage and possible Caracciolo completions, then contrasts France and Japan.

segments.title: Minimal carrier studies train designers without promising ships
segments.summary: The Royal Navy's 17,000-ton carrier papers were exploratory design work, not evidence of an approved peacetime building program.
segments.body: A medium carrier could provide only a minimal air group or minimal protection, leaving the Admiralty to examine both smaller emergency designs and larger fleet ships. The real value of difficult paper studies was educational: generations of constructors learned how tonnage, protection, and aviation facilities interacted, later informing Unicorn and light fleet carriers.
segments.question: Where do claims about planned 17,000-ton Royal Navy carriers come from?
segments.answerShort: They come from design studies rather than an active construction plan; the studies explored tradeoffs and helped train constructors for later carrier projects.

evidence.note: The transcript distinguishes the 17,000-ton option from preferred carrier sizes.
The transcript explains the documentary and educational status of carrier design studies.

segments.title: Contingency plans do not prove a likely war
segments.summary: Interwar plans for Anglo-American conflict reflect professional prudence against the only peer fleet, not a government expectation that war was approaching.
segments.body: A first sea lord or American planner would be negligent to ignore the only navy capable of matching his own, however friendly relations appeared. Dr. Clarke warns that official histories can mistake contingency files and unbuilt constructor studies for political intent or approved procurement.
segments.question: Do British and American war plans show that conflict between them was considered likely?
segments.answerShort: No. Each navy needed a contingency against its only peer opponent, but keeping such a plan is different from expecting or preparing politically for war.

evidence.note: The transcript critiques using contingency plans and paper studies as proof of war likelihood or construction intent.

segments.title: Alternate history works best near the point of divergence
segments.summary: The scenario stops before the 1930 treaty and the Second World War because uncertainty multiplies rapidly beyond the first treaty settlement.
segments.body: A disciplined counterfactual changes one personality, follows evidence through the immediate consequences, and explains each causal step. The resulting Washington settlement and early ship programs are arguable; detailed claims about the next treaty or wartime operations would outrun what the evidence can support.

evidence.note: The transcript explains the evidentiary limits and teaching value of alternate-history exercises.

segments.title: How to read Roskill on interwar naval policy
segments.summary: Roskill remains valuable, but his construction and strategy claims need comparison with constructor records and the limits of his interview network.
segments.body: Many senior officers and technical decision-makers were dead before Roskill wrote, and some left poor records. His official position gave him access and trust, but did not ensure that every surviving informant understood the Third Sea Lord's design process.
segments.question: Can Roskill's interwar naval policy history still be trusted if its carrier claims are wrong?
segments.answerShort: Yes, but construction sections and some strategic conclusions should be checked against technical archives and the perspectives Roskill could not interview.

evidence.note: The transcript directly assesses Roskill's strengths and source limitations.

segments.title: Reserve fleets preserve useful second-line ships
segments.summary: An eclipsed reserve ship can move to second-line duties until the next generation enters reserve and makes it genuinely disposable.
segments.body: Fast technical change did not instantly make every older hull useless. Pre-dreadnoughts still performed convoy escort, shore bombardment, and other wartime tasks, while navies cycled newer ships into reserve and retired the oldest generation.
segments.question: What happens when ships become outdated while sitting in reserve?
segments.answerShort: They remain available for secondary roles until newer reserve ships replace them, after which the oldest hulls can be discarded.

evidence.note: The transcript answers how reserve generations are cycled and lists continued uses for old ships.

segments.title: Versailles leaves Germany without a carrier industry
segments.summary: Early German carrier construction would require reversing both economic restrictions and Britain's effort to dismantle Germany's maritime industrial capability.
segments.body: Treaty damage to yards, skills, and design capacity forced later German warships toward costly and inefficient solutions. Rearmament then prioritized army and air force spending, leaving the navy a distant third and preventing rapid expansion of the infrastructure needed for carriers or multiple modern capital ships.
segments.question: What would Germany need in order to afford and build aircraft carriers early?
segments.answerShort: A radically different Versailles settlement preserving both the German economy and its maritime industrial base, followed by much greater priority for naval infrastructure.

evidence.note: The transcript identifies Versailles, maritime-industrial damage, infrastructure limits, and army-air force priority as the central barriers.

segments.title: All-forward guns save weight but concentrate risk
segments.summary: The Royal Navy favored balanced gun layouts when tonnage allowed because separated magazines preserved fighting power after damage.
segments.body: Concentrating every main turret forward is efficient when designers must maximize armament under a hard displacement limit. A conventional fore-and-aft layout can split magazines into independent groups, so flooding or explosion in one area does not necessarily silence the entire main battery.
segments.question: Why did the Royal Navy take so long to return to an all-forward main-gun layout?
segments.answerShort: All-forward armament is a tonnage-saving compromise; when displacement is available, a balanced layout offers better magazine separation and damage resilience.

evidence.note: The transcript answers the layout question by contrasting fixed-tonnage efficiency with magazine subdivision.

segments.title: Soviet participation breaks the treaty ratio system
segments.summary: Giving the Soviet Union a treaty ratio creates an irreconcilable status problem for Japan and a new fleet-allocation problem for Moscow.
segments.body: The Soviet Union would resist equality with France or Italy, but Japan would reject Soviet parity after fighting Russia and facing it across East Asia. Moscow would also have to divide any allowance among the Baltic, Black Sea, Northern, and Pacific fleets; even a few modern Soviet capital ships would force Germany to divert scarce resources eastward.
segments.question: How would Soviet entry into the naval treaties affect the Baltic, Black Sea, and wider balance?
segments.answerShort: It would destabilize the ratio negotiations, alarm Japan, stretch Soviet tonnage across several fleets, and force Germany to plan against an additional capital-ship threat.

evidence.note: The transcript lays out the Japan-Soviet ratio problem and Soviet multi-fleet allocation.
The transcript traces the consequences of even limited Soviet construction for Germany and Japan.

segments.title: Allied shipbuilding does not rescue Germany's economy
segments.summary: Naval stimulus could cushion Britain or America without preventing Germany's export crisis and political collapse.
segments.body: Shipbuilding wages would circulate mainly within the countries placing orders, and governments became more protectionist after the crash. Germany's export-dependent economy would still lose foreign markets even if Britain and America maintained more domestic industrial employment.
segments.question: Could stronger British shipbuilding soften the crash enough to prevent Hitler's rise?
segments.answerShort: Probably not, because the stimulus would remain domestic while protectionism still destroyed the export markets on which Germany depended.

evidence.note: The transcript directly separates domestic naval stimulus from Germany's export and protectionism problems.

segments.title: Carriers already serve the decisive-battle strategy
segments.summary: More carriers strengthen Japanese attrition operations without replacing the decisive battle at the end of the campaign plan.
segments.body: Kantai Kessen names the climactic engagement rather than the whole Japanese strategy. Carrier strikes already belonged to the process of wearing down an approaching enemy fleet before that final battle, so extra flight decks amplify an existing role.
segments.question: Would more Japanese carriers become a larger part of the Kantai Kessen strategy?
segments.answerShort: They would strengthen the pre-battle attrition phase, but carriers were already integrated into the wider strategy that culminated in the decisive battle.

evidence.note: The transcript distinguishes Kantai Kessen's decisive engagement from the preceding attrition campaign and places carriers in both.

segments.title: Peacetime standards make tiny carriers impractical
segments.summary: Wartime escort carriers fit below 10,000 tons by accepting merchant construction standards that peacetime navies would reject.
segments.body: British studies repeatedly struggled to combine a useful air group, survivability, and military construction below the threshold. Wartime urgency later justified putting a flight deck on a merchant-type hull, but that compromise was not acceptable for a normal peacetime program.
segments.question: Why could the Royal Navy build small wartime escort carriers but not a viable sub-10,000-ton peacetime carrier?
segments.answerShort: Wartime escort carriers accepted merchant-hull standards and emergency compromises that a peacetime warship was expected to avoid.

evidence.note: The transcript contrasts peacetime military construction with wartime merchant-hull escort carriers.

segments.title: A larger Dorsetshire needs more than a gun change
segments.summary: Changing Dorsetshire to 9.2-inch guns does not by itself determine her torpedo attack on Bismarck.
segments.body: Torpedo employment depends on the reconstructed cruiser's displacement, speed, weapons fit, and tactical position, not simply its gun caliber. A sound counterfactual must specify whether the ship remains near 10,000 tons or becomes a much larger design before predicting the attack.
segments.question: Would a 9.2-inch-gun Dorsetshire have fired more torpedoes at Bismarck?
segments.answerShort: There is not enough information: gun caliber alone does not determine torpedo fit or tactics, so the alternative ship's size and full design must be defined first.

evidence.note: The transcript refuses a gun-only prediction and lists the missing design and torpedo variables.

segments.title: Soviet battleships depend on foreign technical links
segments.summary: Treaty participation could give Soviet designers time and foreign relationships to turn paper battleship ambitions into slow domestic construction.
segments.body: The Soviet Union possessed potential yard capacity and maintained technical relationships with Italy, Britain, and the United States, including occasional interest in foreign orders. Starting a program around 1930 would still produce very long building times, but the associated infrastructure could improve later fleet support and maintenance.

evidence.note: The transcript discusses Soviet yard capacity, foreign technical relationships, construction timing, and infrastructure effects.
